verses.text: Bildad scolds Job for endless words and accuses him of tearing himself in anger, as if the earth should be moved for his sake. He resents Job's self-defence. Pride is quick to read another's grief as mere stubborn pride.

Bildad scolds Job for endless words and accuses him of tearing himself in anger, as if the earth should be moved for his sake. He resents Job's self-defence. Pride is quick to read another's grief as mere stubborn pride.

Bildad scolds Job for endless words and accuses him of tearing himself in anger, as if the earth should be moved for his sake. He resents Job's self-defence. Pride is quick to read another's grief as mere stubborn pride.

'The light of the wicked shall be put out,' he says; his own schemes trip him up. Bildad recites the doom of the godless. The principle is true, yet pressed on Job it becomes a verdict God has not given.

'The light of the wicked shall be put out,' he says; his own schemes trip him up. Bildad recites the doom of the godless. The principle is true, yet pressed on Job it becomes a verdict God has not given.

'The light of the wicked shall be put out,' he says; his own schemes trip him up. Bildad recites the doom of the godless. The principle is true, yet pressed on Job it becomes a verdict God has not given.
The wicked man, he says, is caught by his own feet in a net, snares hidden in his path. Sin lays its own traps. The guilty are indeed often ensnared by the very devices they trusted.
The wicked man, he says, is caught by his own feet in a net, snares hidden in his path. Sin lays its own traps. The guilty are indeed often ensnared by the very devices they trusted.
The wicked man, he says, is caught by his own feet in a net, snares hidden in his path. Sin lays its own traps. The guilty are indeed often ensnared by the very devices they trusted.

Terrors hem him in on every side, and 'the firstborn of death' devours his strength. Bildad heaps up images of dread. He means to frighten Job into confession, mistaking affliction for proof of guilt.

Terrors hem him in on every side, and 'the firstborn of death' devours his strength. Bildad heaps up images of dread. He means to frighten Job into confession, mistaking affliction for proof of guilt.

Terrors hem him in on every side, and 'the firstborn of death' devours his strength. Bildad heaps up images of dread. He means to frighten Job into confession, mistaking affliction for proof of guilt.

His confidence is uprooted from his tent and he is marched to 'the king of terrors,' his roots dried below. Bildad pictures the wicked torn from all security. The portrait is grim and, against Job, unjust.

His confidence is uprooted from his tent and he is marched to 'the king of terrors,' his roots dried below. Bildad pictures the wicked torn from all security. The portrait is grim and, against Job, unjust.

His confidence is uprooted from his tent and he is marched to 'the king of terrors,' his roots dried below. Bildad pictures the wicked torn from all security. The portrait is grim and, against Job, unjust.
His memory perishes from the earth, leaving neither son nor survivor among his people. To be utterly forgotten is the dread Bildad foretells. Yet God remembers His own when men forget them entirely.
His memory perishes from the earth, leaving neither son nor survivor among his people. To be utterly forgotten is the dread Bildad foretells. Yet God remembers His own when men forget them entirely.
His memory perishes from the earth, leaving neither son nor survivor among his people. To be utterly forgotten is the dread Bildad foretells. Yet God remembers His own when men forget them entirely.

Those who come after are appalled at his fate; 'such are the dwellings of the wicked,' Bildad concludes. He rests his case on the ruin of the godless. But he never asks whether the man before him is wicked at all.
